attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for her annual health check. She has a medical history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes diagnosed five years ago. Her regular medications include metformin, perindopril, and atorvastatin. She works as a teacher and reports good medication adherence. She has a family history of kidney disease, with her father requiring dialysis in his 70s.

On examination, she appears well, with vital signs within normal limits. You discuss a kidney health check with her.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following comprises the three components of the test?
options: Blood pressure, haemoglobin A1c, serum creatinine
Urine microscopy, blood pressure, kidney ultrasound
24-hour urine collection, kidney ultrasound, serum electrolytes
Estimated glomerular filtration rate, haemoglobin A1c, urine culture
Blood pressure, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, estimated glomerular filtration rate
attributes.explanation:
A kidney health check is an important screening tool for chronic kidney disease (CKD), particularly in patients with risk factors such as diabetes, hypertension, and family history of kidney disease, as in this case. Early detection of CKD allows for interventions that can slow disease progression and reduce complications.

Blood pressure, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, estimated glomerular filtration rate are the three components that comprise a kidney health check. Blood pressure measurement is essential as hypertension is both a cause and consequence of CKD. The urine albumin-creatinine ratio (uACR) detects albuminuria, which is an early marker of kidney damage. The estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), calculated from serum creatinine, assesses kidney function.

Blood pressure, haemoglobin A1c, serum creatinine is incorrect because haemoglobin A1c monitors glycaemic control but is not part of kidney health assessment. While blood pressure and serum creatinine (used to calculate eGFR) are relevant, the complete check requires a uACR.

Urine microscopy, blood pressure, kidney ultrasound is incorrect. Blood pressure is included, but urine microscopy and kidney ultrasound are not routine components of a kidney health check. These tests may be ordered based on specific clinical indications.

24-hour urine collection, kidney ultrasound, serum electrolytes is incorrect. None of these tests are part of the standard kidney health check. A spot (typically morning) urine albumin-creatinine ratio is preferred over 24-hour collection for screening purposes.

Estimated glomerular filtration rate, haemoglobin A1c, urine culture is incorrect. While eGFR is part of a kidney health check, haemoglobin A1c and urine culture are not. Urine culture would be more appropriate to diagnose a urinary tract infections.

In Australia, Kidney Health Australia recommends that people at increased risk of CKD (including those with diabetes, hypertension, established cardiovascular disease, family history of kidney failure, or obesity) should have a kidney health check at least every 1-2 years. For Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, annual kidney health checks are recommended from age 18 onwards due to the higher prevalence of CKD in these populations.
learningPoints: A kidney health check consists of blood pressure measurement, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, and estimated glomerular filtration rate.
Regular kidney health checks are recommended for people at increased risk of chronic kidney disease.
Early detection of chronic kidney disease allows for interventions that can slow disease progression.
references: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) Management in Primary Care (5th edition). Kidney Health Australia. 2024. Available from: https://assets.kidney.org.au/resources/KHA-CKD-Handbook-5th-Ed-July2024.pdf

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
